questions.question: Define the Application Layer in the OSI model and state its main purpose.
keyPoints: Seventh/topmost layer of OSI model
Provides interface for user applications to access network services
Enables communication between software programs across network
Does not provide network routing or transport services directly

questions.question: Explain the difference between client-server and peer-to-peer models with an example of each.
keyPoints: Client-server: Central server provides services to multiple clients (example: Web server, email server)
P2P: All nodes act as both client and server (example: BitTorrent, file sharing networks)
Client-server offers centralized management but single point of failure
P2P offers distributed resources but harder to manage security

questions.question: What is socket programming? Explain the basic steps involved in client-server socket communication.
keyPoints: Socket programming connects two nodes on a network for communication
Server steps: Create socket, Bind to port, Listen for connections, Accept connection
Client steps: Create socket, Connect to server's port
After connection: Send and receive data, Close sockets
Uses TCP (reliable) or UDP (fast but unreliable) protocols

questions.question: List and explain the functions of any four Application Layer protocols.
keyPoints: HTTP: Web communication, defines request/response format
FTP: File transfer between client and server
SMTP: Email sending between mail servers
DNS: Translates domain names to IP addresses
DHCP: Automatic IP address assignment to devices
Each protocol has specific port number and message format

questions.question: What are well-known port numbers? Give three examples with their corresponding protocols.
keyPoints: Well-known ports range from 0-1023
Reserved for standard system services
Examples: Port 80 (HTTP), Port 21 (FTP), Port 25 (SMTP), Port 443 (HTTPS), Port 53 (DNS)
Port numbers allow multiple services to run on same device

questions.question: Explain the difference between stateful and stateless protocols with one example of each.
keyPoints: Stateful: Maintains information about the communication session
Example: TCP - tracks sequence numbers, acknowledges packets
Stateless: Treats each request independently without session memory
Example: HTTP - each request is independent, server doesn't remember previous requests
Trade-off: Stateful is more complex but reliable, stateless is simpler but may need additional mechanisms for session tracking

questions.question: What is an API in the context of network applications? How does it differ from network protocols?
keyPoints: API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of rules for building software applications
Provides abstractions for developers to interact with network services
Network protocols define rules for actual data transmission between devices
APIs work at application level, protocols work at lower OSI layers
APIs make network programming easier by hiding complexity

questions.question: Discuss the Application Layer in detail, covering its position in OSI model, functions, major protocols, and its importance in network communication.
keyPoints: Application Layer is 7th layer, closest to end user
Provides network services to end-user applications
Functions: File transfer, email, web browsing, name resolution, DHCP
Major protocols: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, DHCP, SNMP
Enables user productivity and network services access
Works with transport layer for data transmission
Critical for providing interface between user applications and network infrastructure
Supports various network models: client-server, P2P

questions.question: Explain the client-server model of network application architecture. Also discuss socket programming concepts used to implement client-server communication.
keyPoints: Client-server model: Clients request services, servers provide services
Server: Fixed address, always on, handles multiple client requests
Client: Initiates communication, may connect intermittently
Advantages: Centralized resources, easier security, better scalability
Socket programming: Creates communication endpoints
Server socket: Binds to port, listens, accepts connections
Client socket: Connects to server port
Communication via send() and receive() functions
Example with TCP for reliable communication

questions.question: Compare TCP and UDP transport protocols. Also explain how they are used in application layer communications.
keyPoints: TCP: Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered delivery, flow control, error checking
UDP: Connectionless, unreliable, no ordering, minimal overhead
TCP: Used by HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, SSH (applications needing reliability)
UDP: Used by DNS, DHCP, video streaming (applications needing speed)
TCP three-way handshake for connection establishment
UDP faster but application must handle reliability if needed
Choice depends on application requirements for reliability vs speed

questions.question: What is DNS? Explain how DNS resolution works and discuss its importance in internet communication.
keyPoints: DNS (Domain Name System) translates domain names to IP addresses
Hierarchical system: Root servers, TLD servers, Authoritative servers
Resolution process: Client queries local DNS server, which queries up the hierarchy
Caching reduces lookup time for frequently accessed domains
Types of DNS records: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME, MX, NS
Without DNS, users would need to remember IP addresses instead of names
Critical for scalability of the internet
Port 53 used for DNS communication
